sources.notes: Game on an array; players split composite elements until the array is non-decreasing or no move remains.
Official editorial used for the prime-base classification.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate; not used for this review.

annotation.summary: Given an array splitting game, decide whether Alice can prevent the final array from becoming non-decreasing.
annotation.constraints: Total n <= 1e5 and a_i <= 1e6, so each value can be factorized directly or with a smallest-prime sieve.
annotation.core_idea: If the original array is already non-decreasing, Bob wins. Otherwise classify each value as 1, a prime power's unique prime base, or a multi-prime value. A multi-prime value gives Alice an immediate winning split. If all values are 1 or prime powers, future splits preserve the same prime base, so Bob wins exactly when the prime-base sequence is non-decreasing.
annotation.complexity: O(n sqrt A) by trial division, or O(A log log A + n log A) with sieve preprocessing.

solution_variants.name: primebase-game-classification
solution_variants.summary: Factor each value to its unique prime base or mark it multi-prime, then decide from original sortedness, existence of a multi-prime value, and sortedness of the prime-base sequence.

tags.evidence: The solution is a win/loss classification under optimal play.
The decisive case split depends on whether a value has zero, one, or multiple distinct prime factors.
Prime powers preserve the same underlying prime base under every split.
If Alice can win, the key move is an immediate split exposing a bad small prime factor.
